diagrams.description: This timeline traces the development of Labor Law from early factory regulations to modern international standards. Key milestones: the British Factory Acts (1802‑1833) limiting child labor and working hours; the legalization of trade unions (UK 1871, France 1884); the ILO creation (1919); the New Deal labor legislation in the U.S. (National Labor Relations Act 1935, Fair Labor Standards Act 1938); post‑WWII expansion of social protection; ILO Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work (1998); EU working time and anti‑discrimination directives (1990s‑2000s); and the rise of gig‑economy and platform work regulations in the 2010s‑2020s.
diagrams.mermaid_code:
timeline title History of Labor Law section Early Factory Regulation 1802 : UK Factory Act (first) 1833 : UK Factory Act (inspectors) 1842 : Mines Act (women & children) section Trade Union Recognition 1871 : UK Trade Union Act 1884 : France legalizes unions section ILO & International Standards 1919 : ILO founded 1930 : Forced Labour Convention section U.S. New Deal 1935 : National Labor Relations Act 1938 : Fair Labor Standards Act section Post‑WWII & EU 1951 : Equal Remuneration Convention 1970s : EU social policy begins 1993 : EU Working Time Directive section Fundamental Principles 1998 : ILO Declaration on Fundamental Principles and Rights at Work section Contemporary 2010s : Rise of platform work
and gig economy 2020s : Remote work, AI in
workplace regulation
